##### About me

My birth and postpartum philosophy centers on deeply nurturing the mother as a whole—spiritually, physically, and emotionally—during one of the most transformative seasons of her life. I believe that when a mother is supported, seen, and cared for, she is better able to bond with and care for her baby. I approach each family with compassion, presence, and respect, honoring their unique journey and intuition. Physically, I provide gentle, practical support that promotes rest, recovery, and confidence. Emotionally, I create a safe, nonjudgmental space where mothers can process their experiences and feel truly heard. Spiritually, I hold space for reflection, grounding, and connection, helping mothers feel rooted and empowered as they transition into this new chapter. My goal is to ensure no mother feels alone, but instead held, supported, and strengthened in mind, body, and spirit.

##### Certification and training

Postpartum doula DNT network (2026)

Meditation certification yoga renew (2026)

##### Credentials

CPD

Show less


Show the rest


##### Languages

English, American Sign Language

##### Service introduction

As a postpartum doula, I provide gentle, supportive care to help families transition smoothly during the early weeks with a new baby. My services include caring for the newborn through feeding support, diapering, soothing, and bathing, while also helping older siblings feel supported and included. I assist with light household tasks such as laundry, dishes, and tidying common areas so parents can focus on rest and bonding. I also offer simple meal preparation and attentive care for the mother, ensuring she is nourished, comfortable, and supported physically and emotionally during her postpartum recovery. My goal is to create a calm, nurturing environment where the whole family feels cared for

## About Me

Motherhood was never meant to be lonely. I am a postpartum doula here to help with daytime or overnight support along with sibling care household chores and of course mothering the mother. I was drawn to birth work after withdrawing from nursing school. I was craving a deeper connection with my patients and wanted to help support them for longer than just a 12 hour shift!

The cost of a doula in Tyler, TX can vary depending on the experience and services offered by the doula. On average, the cost of hiring a doula in Tyler, TX can range from $800 to $2000. It is important to research and interview multiple doulas to find one that fits your budget and meets your specific needs.

The cost of a postpartum doula in Tyler, TX can vary widely depending on the individual doula's experience, services offered, and the length of time needed. On average, postpartum doula services in Tyler, TX can range from $25 to $40 per hour. Some doulas also offer package deals for a set number of hours or days. It's best to contact individual doulas in the area to get specific pricing information.

It's important to note that doula fees can vary significantly within the Tyler area, with some highly experienced or specialized doulas charging higher rates. Additionally, some doulas offer package deals that may include prenatal visits, continuous labor support, and postpartum support, which can affect the overall cost.


When considering hiring a doula in Tyler, it's advisable to research and interview several doulas to find one who aligns with your preferences, needs, and budget. During consultations, you can discuss their specific services and pricing structure to ensure you have a clear understanding of what is included and the associated costs. This process will help you find a doula who meets your needs and fits within your budget.

[![Stephanie Harris](https://res.cloudinary.com/bornbir/image/upload/c_thumb,f_auto,g_faces,h_200,w_200/v1/production/99trlx87twmqt2zjd2i8iv3hhkhc.png)\\

Hi, I’m Stephanie, the heart behind Cedar & Honey Birth Co. I’m a birth doula and licensed massage therapist with over a decade of experience supporting moms through pregnancy, birth, and the postpartum season.\\
My approach to birth is calm, steady, and deeply supportive. I believe every mom deserves to feel informed, respected, and well cared for — physically, emotionally, and spiritually — throughout her journey. I offer hands-on comfort measures, evidence-based education, advocacy in hospital settings, and continuous presence so you feel supported and confident during birth.\\
With a background in medical and therapeutic massage, I bring advanced bodywork skills into labor support — helping with comfort, relaxation, positioning, and nervous system regulation. I’m trauma-informed, intuitive, and experienced working alongside healthcare professionals, allowing me to support you confidently whether your birth follows your hopes or takes an unexpected turn.\\
Faith is an important part of who I am, and I naturally integrate prayer, encouragement, and spiritual support into my care. This is woven into the way I serve moms and families, with gentleness, reverence, and trust in God’s presence throughout birth.\\
My heart is to create a peaceful, reassuring space where you feel prepared, supported, and confident as you welcome your baby. It would be an honor to walk this journey with you.](https://www.bornbir.com/stephanie-harris)

[![Alissa January](https://res.cloudinary.com/bornbir/image/upload/c_thumb,f_auto,g_faces,h_200,w_200/v1/production/uihv4o056zwsw9t6toariwcxwbd7.jpeg)\\

Longview, Texas\\

Hey Everyone! My name is Alissa January and I am a certified birth doula in Longview, Tx. However, I serve all of East Texas! My passion is serving mamas through their pregnancy, labor, and postpartum journey! I have a lot of different packages I offer for you to choose from! Book your FREE consultation today on my website and get connected with me to discuss how I can best serve you!](https://www.bornbir.com/alissa-january)

[![Emily Barton](https://res.cloudinary.com/bornbir/image/upload/c_thumb,f_auto,g_faces,h_200,w_200/v1/production/va72o44po0lf0kj3wt3d4g30mcgj.jpeg)\\
\\
Longview, Texas\\
\\
**Emily Barton**\\
\\
Hi, I’m Emily! I’m a mama of four and a birth doula serving families throughout East Texas. Motherhood and birth have always been something deeply important to me, and after having my own children, I knew I wanted to support other women through one of the most vulnerable and powerful seasons of their lives.\\
\\
I truly love what I do. My goal as a doula is to help families feel supported, informed, and cared for throughout pregnancy, labor, birth, and postpartum. I support all types of births and believe every family deserves to feel heard, respected, and empowered in their experience. Whether you’re planning an unmedicated birth, epidural, induction, cesarean, or home birth, I’m there to provide emotional support, education, comfort measures, and encouragement every step of the way.\\
\\
I strive to create a warm, judgment-free space where you can ask questions, feel safe, and know you don’t have to do this alone.](https://www.bornbir.com/emily-barton)

[![Mallory Villafuerte](https://res.cloudinary.com/bornbir/image/upload/c_thumb,f_auto,g_faces,h_200,w_200/v1/production/onn3p813hyz3749moe21ss5uvdb2.jpg)\\
\\
Tyler, Texas\\
\\
**Mallory Villafuerte**\\
\\
4 years of experience\\
\\
\\
Hi, I’m Mallory the heart behind The Crunchy-ish Doula.\\
I’ve been married to my husband for nine years, and together we’re raising three beautiful children: a curious 7 year old, a spirited 4 year old, and our sweet 2 year old. Motherhood has been one of my greatest teachers, and every birth story I carry my own and the ones I’m honored to witness has shaped the way I serve families.\\
I welcomed my first child in a hospital setting. While I’m grateful for that experience, it also opened my eyes to how unprepared and disconnected many women can feel during birth. After that birth, and through the heartache of several miscarriages, I spent a lot of time praying asking the Lord to show me my purpose and how He wanted to use my story.\\
In His perfect timing, God answered a lifelong prayer.\\
After the home birth of my second son, everything became clear. I experienced firsthand the power of education, support, and trust in the body God designed. That birth changed me and in 2021, I became a doula.\\
My calling is to walk alongside families with open hands and a steady presence, honoring both faith and informed decision making. I believe God created our bodies with intention and wisdom, and I also believe there are moments when medical intervention is necessary and life saving. My role is never to push an agenda, but to help you prepare, discern, and advocate with confidence and peace no matter where or how you give birth.\\
Birth is sacred. Mothers are strong. And no family should feel alone in this season.\\
It would be an honor to support you.](https://www.bornbir.com/mallory-villafuerte)

My own transformative experiences as a mother ignited a deep commitment to empower and nurture others during this pivotal chapter of life, leading me to embark on the fulfilling path of doula work.\\
\\
My journey as a birth doula revolves around creating positive and empowering birthing experiences. I'm dedicated to offering emotional encouragement, evidence-based information, and physical comfort to expectant parents as they navigate the intricacies of labor and delivery. Advocating for birthing individuals, I ensure they feel informed, empowered, and supported in making choices aligned with their values and preferences. Seamlessly transitioning into my role as a postpartum doula, I extend support to new parents, providing guidance on newborn care, breastfeeding, and postpartum recovery. My non-judgmental and compassionate approach fosters a secure space, allowing families to thrive during the transformative early days of parenthood.](https://www.bornbir.com/camilla-morgan)

## Doula Services FAQs

### What is a doula and why should you hire one?

A doula is a trained support specialist who provides continuous physical, emotional, and informational support to the mother before, during, and after childbirth. 98.6% of births in the U.S. take place in hospitals, and nearly all of them require some kind of medical intervention. However, doula-supported deliveries are linked to shorter labor, decreased need for medications, fewer c-sections, higher rates of breastfeeding initiation, less anxiety, and less postpartum depression.


### Can you pay for a doula with HSA or FSA Funds in Tyler, TX?

To use HSA or FSA funds for doula services, they generally needed to be considered medically necessary. You might need a doctor's note or prescription stating that doula services are necessary for a specific medical condition. Keep detailed records of payments and any supporting documentation, such as a doctor's note or prescription, to substantiate that the expense was medically necessary.


### How much does a doula cost in Tyler, TX?

The cost of a doula in Tyler, TX can vary depending on the experience and services offered by the doula. On average, the cost of hiring a doula in Tyler, TX can range from $800 to $2000. It is important to research and interview multiple doulas to find one that fits your budget and meets your specific needs.


### Are doulas covered by insurance in Tyler, TX?

Whether insurance covers the cost of a doula in Tyler, TX, depends on your specific insurance plan. Some insurance providers may offer partial or full coverage for doula services, especially if they are considered part of maternity care. It's best to contact your insurance company directly to inquire about coverage and ask if they require specific documentation or a certified doula. Additionally, some doulas may offer payment plans or work with Health Savings Accounts (HSAs) or Flexible Spending Accounts (FSAs) to make their services more affordable.


### What types of doulas are for hire in Tyler, TX?

In Tyler, TX, you can find various types of doulas for hire, including birth doulas, postpartum doulas, and sometimes specialized roles such as antepartum doulas and bereavement doulas. Birth doulas provide emotional, physical, and informational support during labor and delivery, helping parents navigate the birthing process. Postpartum doulas focus on assisting families after birth, offering support with newborn care, breastfeeding, emotional recovery, and household tasks. Antepartum doulas support individuals with high-risk pregnancies or those needing extra care during pregnancy, while bereavement doulas offer compassionate support to families experiencing pregnancy or infant loss. Availability may vary, so it’s recommended to connect with local doula networks or organizations for specific services.


### When should I hire a doula in pregnancy?

You can start researching and contacting doulas as early as your first trimester. This allows you ample time to find a doula who aligns with your birth plan and build a rapport with them. Many people choose to hire a doula during their second trimester, around weeks 12 to 27. By this point, you likely have a clearer understanding of your birth preferences and can engage a doula who can provide support tailored to your needs. If you’re in your third trimester or even approaching your due date, it’s not too late to hire a doula. Some individuals make this decision later in pregnancy due to various reasons, and doulas can still offer valuable support and information during this time. The availability of doulas in your area may also influence when you hire one. In some regions, experienced doulas may have limited availability, so it’s wise to start your search early to secure the support you desire.


### What's the difference between a midwife and a doula?

A midwife is a trained health professional who helps women during labor, delivery, and after the birth of their babies. Midwives can provide gynecological examinations, prenatal care, and postpartum support. They are qualified to deliver babies and are trained to handle certain complications during childbirth. A doula, on the other hand, is a non-medical professional who provides emotional, physical, and educational support to a mother who is expecting, is experiencing labor, or has recently given birth. The doula's role is to help women have a safe, memorable, and empowering birthing experience. They are known for their continuous support, beginning during pregnancy and lasting through the postpartum period. While midwives have a broad scope of practice that is clinical in nature, doulas specialize in the non-clinical aspects of care. They might be preferred by mothers who are looking for continuous bedside support that is not typically provided by the medical staff. Doulas are known for their advocacy for the mother's wishes during childbirth, which can be especially valuable in hospital settings where the mother may desire a birth plan that deviates from standard procedures.

Bornbir is entirely free for new and expecting parents to use. To begin, simply tell our community of doulas what you need in your job posting and let the right providers come to you. web.title: In Defence of Midwives, Mothers, and the Truth - Open Letter
web.description: We write as midwives, doulas, educators, and advocates for families - united by our terror for the future of birth in the UK. We have watched ...
web.markdown:
**10 November 2025**

An Open Letter on Birth: In Defence of Midwives, Mothers, and the Truth

From: Midwives, Doulas, and Educators across the UK (written by The People's Birth Tribunal)

To: Parents, Professionals, Politicians, and the Press

Date: October 2025

Introduction

This open letter has been written and signed by midwives, doulas, and educators working across the United Kingdom.

Together, we represent those who stand beside women and families through pregnancy, birth, and the postnatal period - the people who have witnessed both the miracles and the failings of our maternity system firsthand.

We are speaking out because the public conversation about birth in the UK has become dangerously distorted. Misleading headlines, sensational stories, and selective statistics have created a culture of fear - fear of birth, fear of accountability, and fear of trusting women, birthing people and midwives.

It is time to set the record straight: Maternity care must prioritise the health and well-being of the whole family, ensuring good mental, physical and emotional health and protecting reproductive rights, thus creating a foundation upon which parents and children can thrive.

Clinical safety is just as important: preventing mortality and severe morbidity is, of course, crucial in high quality maternity care. However, prioritising clinical, risk-focussed management over holistic, community and relationship-based care can, and does, introduce other dangers to the well-being of the whole family, both in the short and long terms.

We write as midwives, doulas, educators, and advocates for families - united by our terror for the future of birth in the UK.

We have watched with growing alarm as midwives and the concept of “normal birth” have been scapegoated for the failings of a broken maternity system. Recent headlines have twisted complex truths into simple soundbites, heaping blame and shame on midwives, doulas and the families we support. This is eroding public trust and demonising and demoralising the very people who dedicate their lives to supporting women and babies safely.

Let us be clear: there has never been a campaign for “normal birth at any cost.”

This phrase - coined by journalists - is a distortion. It ignores what midwifery actually stands for: individualised, woman-centred, evidence-based care. The Royal College of Midwives’ 2017 decision to retire the term “normal birth” was not a rejection of physiology, but a response to relentless media misrepresentation (RCM, 2017). Yet the damage has continued: each new article fuels the myth that advocating for natural birth is dangerous, even extremist.

Here is the reality:

The safest maternity systems in the world protect physiological birth while providing excellent access to medical care when needed (Hollowell et al., 2011; WHO, 2018).

Induction, instrumental delivery, and caesarean section rates have risen sharply — yet outcomes for women and babies are not improving (MBRRACE-UK, 2023).

Over-medicalisation carries its own risks: physical, emotional, and psychological (NICE, 2021; Walsh, 2020).

Midwives today are working under impossible pressures — short-staffed, over-regulated, and afraid to trust their skills or their intuition (RCM, 2022; Ockenden, 2022).

And here is the human cost:

Parents are entering labour frightened and uninformed. Families are grieving the loss of agency and dignity in birth.

Midwifery students are leaving the profession, feeling that their education no longer reflects the art and science of physiological birth (NMC, 2023).

Midwives themselves - once the guardians of safe, calm, supported birthing - are burning out, leaving, or being silenced (RCM, 2023).

We are tired of seeing care distorted by fear and litigation. We are appalled as we watch policy being driven by headlines instead of evidence. We are tired of the false divide between “safe” and “natural,” when safety has always meant right care, right time, right place.

It is time to reclaim the truth:

Physiological birth is not ideology; it is biology.

Trusting women’s bodies and choices is not reckless; it is human rights-based care (WHO, 2018; White Ribbon Alliance, 2011).

Midwives and doulas are not the problem; we are part of the solution to a system in crisis.

We call on the media to stop vilifying midwifery and start reporting responsibly on the evidence.

We call on policymakers to listen to the voices of women, families, and front-line professionals.

We call on the NHS and education providers to restore balance - to teach and protect physiological birth alongside life-saving medical skills.

And we call on the public to join us in demanding maternity care that is safe and kind, modern and humane.

Birth is not a battleground between ideology and intervention

It is a moment of profound human vulnerability and strength - a time when every woman and birthing person deserves to be seen, heard, and supported in their choices.

We stand together - midwives, doulas, birth educators and lactation specialists - for truth, for trust, and for the future of birth.

To our readers

If you share our concern for the future of birth in the UK:

Share this letter widely — with journalists, MPs, and community leaders.

Ask your maternity service how it supports physiological, evidence-based care.

Listen to and believe women’s experiences of birth.

Together, we can rebuild a maternity system that values safety, compassion, and autonomy in equal measure.

References

Hollowell, J. et al. (2011). The Birthplace in England Research Programme: Perinatal and maternal outcomes by planned place of birth. Oxford: National Perinatal Epidemiology Unit.

MBRRACE-UK (2023). Saving Lives, Improving Mothers’ Care: Lessons learned to inform maternity care from the UK and Ireland Confidential Enquiries into Maternal Deaths and Morbidity 2019–2021. University of Oxford.

NICE (2021). Intrapartum Care for Healthy Women and Babies (NG190). National Institute for Health and Care Excellence.

This letter is signed in an individual capacity. The views and opinions expressed do not necessarily reflect that of any organisation they are associated with or employed by.

**Update06 January 2026**

## Parents - A Call Out

We need parents, grandparents and family members who see the changes happening across UK maternity care to add their weight to this message.

Your voices are important to this campaign. Maternity care is for you, your family, your babies and grandchildren.

Whether you are currently pregnant, have birthed recently, or are simply an advocate for the next generation, your signature signifies that you demand better, and believe there is a future where midwifery is protected and funded appropriately.

​We are asking you to sign the Open Letter if you believe:

​In Autonomy: That the mother or birthing person is the ultimate decision-maker. ​

In Support: That midwives must be supported to practice true midwifery, not just obstetric nursing.

​In Transparency: That parents deserve the full truth about the risks and benefits of all interventions and help to make safe decisions.

**Update06 January 2026**
